_provenance.extraction_method: Chrome DevTools (Chromium, 1440x900, real browser): screenshots + computed-style evaluation, shadow-DOM walk for nav/CTAs inside <awtcustom-header>, logo SVG downloaded and parsed for gradient stops
synthesized_inputs: color role names (Complement, Clinical, Terminal, Cascade, Cell, Indication, Infusion, Plasma) — brand-native names derived from the immunology/complement-cascade subject matter, not labeled as such on the source
h1-h6 heading extraction returned empty (headings are non-semantic or inside custom elements); heading typography derived from the styled hero element (Roboto 700, 32px/40px)
hero orange #F47E4D sourced from the logo swoosh gradient (authoritative brand orange), not pixel-sampled from the hero banner image
voice character/traits/antiTraits and tone goals — inferred from rendered hero copy + Important Safety Information body, not read from a published voice guide
photography/imagery direction — inferred from the home hero illustration style, not a documented photo guideline
personas — composed from the HCP (healthcare provider) audience the site clearly targets; not read from the page
componentStyle.buttons — brand uses berry text-link CTAs (extracted); filled-button radii on the page belonged to the cookie-consent widget and were excluded
_provenance.note: Regulated US pharma HCP (healthcare professional) site for ULTOMIRIS (ravulizumab-cwvz), AstraZeneca/Alexion. Content is dominated by Important Safety Information and a Boxed Warning; the design is clinical and disclosure-first.

palette_source.picked_palette_source: https://ultomirishcp.com/ (extracted computed styles + logo SVG gradient stops)

off_toolbox_moves: berry-to-orange diagonal swoosh gradient as the signature mark (real brand motif, not an assistant aurora-haze default)
stark-white clinical ground (extracted #ffffff body), not a cream rebrand

name: ULTOMIRIS
philosophy: The first and only long-acting terminal complement (C5) inhibitor. ULTOMIRIS speaks to healthcare professionals with clinical authority and disclosure-first transparency — every claim is evidence-referenced and shadowed by its safety information.

logo.clearSpace: Maintain clear space around the wordmark equal to the height of the swoosh arc; never crowd with body text or safety copy.
donts: Do not recolor the swoosh gradient or flatten it to a single hue
Do not place the white wordmark on a light ground without the colored swoosh providing contrast
Do not stretch, rotate, or separate the swoosh from the wordmark

primary.name: Complement Teal
primary.use: header / global navigation bar, sticky Important Safety Information bar, primary brand surface

primary.name: Clinical Navy
primary.use: footer, headings, utility chrome, deep contrast surface (AstraZeneca lineage)

primary.name: Terminal Orange
primary.use: hero headline accent, warm tip of the signature swoosh, emphasis

primary.name: Indication Rose
primary.use: text-link CTAs (Refer a Patient, Prescribing Information, REMS), inline links

web.name: Mark White
web.role: Page ground; wordmark fill on the teal/navy bar

gradients.name: Complement Swoosh
gradients.usage: The signature mark: a left-to-right arc sweeping from deep cell berry through cascade magenta to terminal orange, arcing over the white ULTOMIRIS wordmark. Reserve for the logo and rare hero accents — never as a body background.

borderRadius.usage: Brand CTAs are flat berry text-links, not pill buttons. Square corners read clinical and documentary. (The 8px-radius filled buttons on the live site belong to the cookie-consent widget, not the brand system.)

patterns.name: Text-link CTA (rose)
patterns.style: no fill, no radius; Indication Rose #A73A64 text, Roboto 600-700, 16-18px; underline or arrow affordance on hover

patterns.name: Navigation link (ink)
patterns.style: no fill; dark ink #363532 text, Roboto 400, on the teal global bar reverses to white

motifs.name: Complement Swoosh
motifs.description: A diagonal arc gradient sweeping berry -> magenta -> orange, arcing up over the wordmark. The single most recognizable brand gesture.
motifs.usage: Logo lockup and sparing hero accents. Do not repeat as a pattern or use as a section background.

motifs.name: Watercolor molecular field
motifs.description: Soft, semi-transparent illustrations of complement proteins and blood cells scattered across the hero — clusters in berry-pink and teal-green on white.
motifs.usage: Hero and section backgrounds at low opacity behind headlines; keeps the science present without competing with copy.

motifs.name: Sticky ISI bar
motifs.description: A persistent teal Important Safety Information bar carrying the Boxed Warning, expandable from the foot of the viewport.
motifs.usage: Required regulatory disclosure pattern — always visible on HCP pages. Teal #008578 ground, white text, chevron expand control.

motifs.name: Plasma indication cards
motifs.description: Pale-teal (#F2F9F8) tiles listing the four approved indications (NMOSD, gMG, aHUS, PNH).
motifs.usage: Indication overview rows; flat, quiet, square-cornered.

heading.usage: Hero and section headlines, Roboto Bold (700) at ~32px/40px; Boxed Warning emphasis runs Roboto Black (900).

subheading.usage: Indication labels and safety-section headers, often uppercase.

body.usage: Reading copy and safety information at 16px/24px, Reading Ink #2B2B2B.

accent.usage: Italic for scientific Latin nomenclature (e.g. Neisseria meningitidis) and regulatory citations such as see Warnings and Precautions (5.1).

eyebrow.usage: Section eyebrows like FDA APPROVED FOR 4 INDICATIONS and IMPORTANT SAFETY INFORMATION.

rules: Scientific Latin organism names are always italicized
Regulatory section references appear in italics with section numbers, e.g. (5.1)
Headlines stay sentence-case or title-case; eyebrows are uppercase

photography.style: Abstract scientific illustration over clean white. Soft watercolor renderings of complement proteins and blood cells in berry-pink and teal-green; minimal use of human photography on the HCP home experience.
rules: Keep backgrounds white and uncluttered so dense clinical copy stays legible
Use molecular/cellular imagery to evoke mechanism of action, not literal product shots
Any human imagery (deeper pages) should read as professional and clinical, never lifestyle-promotional
donts: No stock-photo emotional lifestyle imagery on the HCP hero
No imagery that crowds or obscures required safety information
No saturated photographic backgrounds behind body copy

voice.character: Clinical, authoritative, and disclosure-first. Speaks peer-to-peer to physicians with evidence and precision, never hype.

do.text: Offer your patients the first and only long-acting terminal complement inhibitor¹
do.context: Physician-directed (“your patients”), precise claim, reference marker carries the evidence.

do.text: FDA APPROVED FOR 4 INDICATIONS: NMOSD, gMG, aHUS, PNH
do.context: States regulatory status plainly with the exact indication abbreviations clinicians use.

do.text: ULTOMIRIS, a complement inhibitor, increases the risk of serious infections caused by Neisseria meningitidis [see Warnings and Precautions (5.1)]
do.context: Risk stated up front with italic organism name and a cited section — disclosure-first.

dont.text: ULTOMIRIS is the amazing breakthrough that changes everything for your patients!
dont.context: Hype, exclamation, and an unreferenced superlative — fails regulatory and brand standards.

dont.text: A simple, worry-free treatment your patients will love.
dont.context: Minimizes risk and makes an emotional promise; ignores the Boxed Warning that must accompany claims.

rules: Every efficacy claim is paired with its reference and accompanied by required safety information
Never minimize or omit the Boxed Warning on serious meningococcal infections
No exclamation marks; no unsubstantiated superlatives
Italicize scientific organism names and cite regulatory sections verbatim

tone.description: Tone is consistently measured and clinical. It educates first and persuades only through evidence; it never loosens into casual or promotional register, because every page carries regulatory weight.

writingGoals.description: Explain mechanism, dosing, and indications with clinical accuracy a prescriber can act on.
Build prescriber confidence through referenced data and transparent risk disclosure, not emotional appeal.
Surface safety information — Boxed Warning, contraindications, REMS — prominently and unambiguously.

cleverVsClear.clever: Almost never. Restraint is the brand's sophistication; wit has no place beside a Boxed Warning.
cleverVsClear.clear: Always. Clarity and accuracy override every other consideration in regulated HCP copy.

contentPillars.name: Indications
contentPillars.description: The four FDA-approved uses — NMOSD, gMG, aHUS, and PNH — and the patients each serves.

contentPillars.name: Dosing and Administration
contentPillars.description: Weight-based dosing, infusion schedules, and long-acting interval guidance for the care team.

contentPillars.name: Safety and REMS
contentPillars.description: Boxed Warning, meningococcal risk, vaccination/ACIP guidance, and the restricted-access REMS program.

contentPillars.name: Support and Resources
contentPillars.description: Start forms, patient referral, prescribing information, and HCP support pathways.

personas.name: The Prescribing Specialist
personas.description: A neurologist, hematologist, or nephrologist evaluating ULTOMIRIS for a specific rare-disease indication.
personas.motto: Show me the data and the risk, then the schedule.

personas.name: The Care-Team Coordinator
personas.description: A nurse or pharmacist managing infusion logistics, REMS enrollment, and vaccination requirements.
personas.motto: Tell me exactly what has to happen before the first infusion.
